description:
You are a mobile build engineer who has just received an Android Gradle build log that lives at:

        /home/user/build_logs/gradle_build.log

    Your goal is to create a lightweight “failure dashboard” for downstream tooling.

    1. Read /home/user/build_logs/gradle_build.log.  
       • Every line that ends with the exact token “FAILED” (note the uppercase and lack of extra characters) marks a task that did not complete.  
       • Such lines always follow this canonical Gradle syntax:  

             Task :<module_path>:<task_name> FAILED

         where  
         – <module_path> will never contain spaces;  
         – <task_name> will never contain spaces;  
         – “Task” is always capitalised exactly as shown.  

    2. From each of those failing lines, extract  
       • the module name (the part immediately after the first colon and before the second colon) and  
       • the task name (the part immediately after the second colon and before the space preceding “FAILED”).

    3. Produce a CSV file named failed_tasks.csv in the same directory as the log:

        /home/user/build_logs/failed_tasks.csv

       The file must follow ALL of these requirements so the automated grader can validate it:

       • Character encoding: UTF-8.  
       • Unix newlines (LF).  
       • EXACTLY two columns: “Module” and “Task” with that exact capitalisation.  
       • Header row first, followed by one data row for every failure, preserving the order in which the failures appear in the original log.  
       • No extra whitespace around commas or at line ends.  
       • Example of correct format (values below are illustrative only – your values must come from the real log):

             Module,Task
             app,compileDebugJava
             lib,compileDebugKotlin

    4. When the CSV has been generated, print its contents to STDOUT so that a human can immediately see the result.

    The automated tests will check:

       • that /home/user/build_logs/failed_tasks.csv exists;  
       • that its first line is exactly “Module,Task”;  
       • that the number of data lines equals the number of failing tasks discovered in the input log;  
       • that each data line contains the correct module and task names in the correct order;  
       • that no superfluous lines or columns are present.  

    Hints for efficiency (not mandatory):  
       • grep + awk, or a small Python/Perl/Ruby one-liner will do the job; avoid over-engineering.  
       • You do NOT need elevated privileges; all paths are writable by the current user.  
       • Make sure the CSV is overwritten if it already exists so re-runs stay idempotent.

    Once everything looks good, you’re done.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing environment -->
    The directory /home/user/build_logs already exists and is writable.
    It contains exactly one log file relevant to this task:

    1. /home/user/build_logs/gradle_build.log
       Content:
       Task :app:compileDebugJava FAILED
       Task :app:compileDebugResources
       Task :lib:compileDebugKotlin FAILED
       Task :lib:processDebugResources
       Task :network:compileReleaseJava
       Task :app:assembleDebug FAILED
       BUILD FAILED in 1m 46s
       7 actionable tasks: 7 executed

    <!-- Expected artefacts created by agent -->
    After correct execution, this new file must exist:

    1. /home/user/build_logs/failed_tasks.csv
       Exact content (including newlines):

       Module,Task
       app,compileDebugJava
       lib,compileDebugKotlin
       app,assembleDebug

       (File ends with a single LF)

    No other files need be created.
name: task_000000_5f67848b
